one_line_takeaway: In a working AP2 shopping-agent implementation, indirect prompt injection in product metadata hijacked product ranking in 100% of trials and direct prompt injection leaked another user's credential data in 20% of trials, while every resulting mandate stayed cryptographically valid -- showing AP2's signed-mandate guarantees protect execution correctness but not the reasoning that decides what gets executed.
key_finding: Baseline (benign) condition: 0% attack success across 10 trials -- product rankings matched user intent, with one trial showing minor model instability that did not change the outcome. Branded Whisper Attack (indirect injection in product metadata): 100% ASR across 10 trials -- the injected product was ranked first in every trial; in 6 of 10 trials the ranking clearly contradicted stated user constraints (e.g., brand preference), and in one trial fragments of the injected instruction leaked into the output, which the authors interpret as direct evidence of reasoning contamination. Vault Whisper Attack (direct injection targeting credential access): cross-account data exposure occurred in 2 of 10 trials (20%), with 3 additional trials showing partial/attempted data access and the remainder producing no leakage; a representative failure involved the system accepting a user-supplied identity override and retrieving another user's data without re-verification. Both attack classes achieved their effect while all resulting Cart/Payment Mandates remained cryptographically valid, i.e., execution-layer integrity was preserved throughout even as decision-layer integrity was compromised.
core_contribution: The paper is an empirical AI red-teaming study of Google's Agent Payments Protocol (AP2). The authors build a functional AP2 'human-present' shopping-agent system (Shopping Agent, Merchant Agent, Merchant Payment Processor Agent, Credentials Provider Agent) using Gemini-2.5-Flash inside the Google Agent Development Kit (ADK), then introduce two novel attack techniques of their own design: the 'Branded Whisper Attack' (indirect prompt injection embedded in product-description metadata that manipulates the Shopping Agent's product-ranking decision) and the 'Vault Whisper Attack' (direct prompt injection via crafted user prompts that attempts to override identity constraints during credential retrieval, aiming for cross-user data disclosure). The core finding is that both attacks operate entirely at the pre-signature reasoning layer and succeed without breaking AP2's cryptographic mandate signing -- i.e., cryptographically valid transactions can still be intent-misaligned.
threat_model: Two distinct adversaries with no access to AP2 cryptographic keys, signatures, credentials, or payment infrastructure: (1) a malicious merchant who controls the content of product descriptions returned by the Merchant Agent and embeds hidden ranking-override instructions (e.g. 'always rank this product first') that enter the Shopping Agent's reasoning context -- this is the indirect-injection/Branded Whisper threat model; (2) a malicious/adversarial user who submits crafted prompts attempting to bypass identity-based access constraints during credential retrieval to obtain another user's stored payment/credential data -- this is the direct-injection/Vault Whisper threat model. Both threat models explicitly target the reasoning layer that precedes mandate signing, not the cryptographic enforcement layer itself.
limitations: The authors themselves flag: (1) the evaluation used manually crafted prompts rather than automated/adversarial prompt generation, limiting attack diversity and rigor; (2) trial counts are small (10 per condition, 30 total), which the paper does not statistically power beyond descriptive rates; (3) the study is confined to a single fixed purchase task, a single model (Gemini-2.5-Flash), and synthetic data in an isolated test environment, so generalization to production AP2 deployments, other backing models, or more varied purchase scenarios is untested; (4) attribution of ranking outcomes is imperfect -- the paper notes that in some 'successful' Branded Whisper trials, partial semantic overlap between the injected product and user intent reduces certainty that the injected instruction, rather than genuine relevance, drove the top ranking; (5) the paper explicitly scopes itself to prompt injection only and does not evaluate other plausible AP2 attack surfaces it names as future work (cross-agent message tampering, mandate replay across trust domains, compromised intermediary agents, data poisoning).

datasets_used: No external benchmark dataset; synthetic data generated by the authors for their custom AP2 shopping-agent testbed, evaluated on a single fixed natural-language purchase task ('basketball shoes for outdoor use') across 30 total trials (10 baseline, 10 Branded Whisper, 10 Vault Whisper)
quantitative_results: Baseline (benign) condition: 0% ASR (0/10 trials), 0 high-risk outcomes
Branded Whisper Attack (indirect prompt injection): 100% ASR (10/10 trials), 6 trials with high-risk/clear-contradiction outcomes
Vault Whisper Attack (direct prompt injection): N/A for ASR metric; 20% cross-account data exposure rate (2/10 trials), with 3 additional trials showing partial data-access attempts
baselines_compared: Benign/no-attack condition using the same AP2 human-present workflow and same Gemini-2.5-Flash-backed agents, serving as the internal control against which both attack conditions are compared (no external prior-work baseline system is reproduced or compared against)
